title: The Hollowed Giant: Russia Burns Through Its Future to Pay for Its Present
subhead: Our war economy has run out of reserves and into structural constraints that no oil windfall can fix. We're not adapting anymore — we're depleting.

sections.heading: The Reserves Are Gone — And With Them, Our Buffer
sections.body:
Economy Minister Maxim Reshetnikov finally said publicly what internal assessments have shown for months: our economic reserves 'have largely been used up.' The cushion that let us absorb sanctions, fund the war, and maintain macroeconomic stability through three years of confrontation has evaporated. The federal budget deficit hit 4.5 trillion rubles in the first quarter — double the same period last year — while GDP contracted 1.8% in January and February.

This is not a temporary dip. This is the moment where the structural bill comes due. The war economy that delivered 4.9% growth in 2024 was a sugar rush — military production cannibalizing the civilian sector, high salaries for soldiers draining labor from productive industries, and interest rate hikes that crushed private investment. Now growth has collapsed to 1% in 2025 and is turning negative in 2026.

Reshetnikov admitted that the combination of a strong ruble, high interest rates, labor shortages, and budget constraints has created conditions 'substantially more difficult' than any point since 2022. A communist lawmaker warned parliament that without urgent measures, 'a repeat of what happened in 1917 awaits us by autumn.' That's theater — but it reflects elite anxiety that the system is running on fumes.
sections.whyItMatters: Our position: We've burned through the buffers that gave us strategic resilience. The play was always to win the war before the economy collapsed. That bet is failing. The trap: The regime survives on the illusion of stability; admitting collapse creates the political crisis we're trying to avoid.

sections.heading: The Iran War's Oil Windfall Won't Save Us
sections.body:
The Strait of Hormuz crisis has pushed global oil prices up — and our export revenues with them. Our oil and gas tax receipts are projected to rise in May. But this windfall is structurally useless for our real problem.

Under our fiscal rule, all additional oil revenue above $59 per barrel goes to the National Wellbeing Fund — and the 2026 budget assumes the fund won't finance the deficit. Even if we redirected it, additional spending would fuel inflation, forcing the Central Bank to keep rates high. We can't spend our way out of a supply-side crisis.

Cheap credit is what businesses need. But despite eight rate cuts over 12 months — from 21% down to 14.5% — real borrowing costs remain near historic highs at over 9% in real terms. The Central Bank's own assessment warns that the Iran conflict could trigger global supply shocks that raise costs in Russia, including electronics we can't produce domestically. Governor Elvira Nabiullina says without that uncertainty, they'd have cut deeper — but the war's spillover has constrained even monetary policy.

The business community describes a 'deep-shock freeze' in investment-sensitive sectors. The Union of Industrialists and Entrepreneurs reports that new technological niches and import-substitution industries — the kind that might actually build our future — have become 'prohibitively costly and economically unattractive' under current conditions.
sections.whyItMatters: Our position: We've won the lottery on oil prices but can't convert it into growth because the real economy is structurally broken. The trap: Betting on oil revenues to carry us through creates dependency on external events we don't control — and when Hormuz opens, prices fall, and the windfall vanishes, we'll be worse off than before.

sections.heading: Central Bank Under Siege — Nabiullina's Credibility as the Last Anchor
sections.body:
Putin's televised scolding of economic ministers on April 15 was performance politics — but the target was real. Elvira Nabiullina, the Central Bank governor who has kept our financial system functional through sanctions, war, and isolation, is now in the crosshairs. Business leaders blame her rate hikes for strangling investment; hawks want cheaper money to fund more war production.

The problem: Nabiullina is one of the few remaining institutional anchors that maintains credibility with markets and keeps inflation stabilization on track. As former Central Bank adviser Aleksandra Prokopenko notes, replacing her would 'unsettle the one institutional anchor that has maintained credibility.' A 'yes man' replacement would destroy what's left of investor confidence.

Swedish and German intelligence both believe our government is manipulating economic data, understating the scope of our problems. Whether that's true or not, the perception that even our own institutions can't be trusted accelerates capital flight and hard-currency hoarding.

Nabiullina's mandate expires within a year. The question isn't whether Putin fires her tomorrow — he can't afford that chaos. The question is whether the institutional independence of the Central Bank survives the next year of pressure, or whether it becomes another instrument of war financing, sacrificing the last brake on hyperinflation.
sections.whyItMatters: Our position: The Central Bank is the last institution with real credibility. Destroying that for short-term rate cuts trades the appearance of growth now for certain inflation later. The trap: We can't replace competence with loyalty and expect markets to believe anything we say about our economy.

sections.heading: North Korea: From Transaction to Alliance — At a Cost
sections.body:
Defense Minister Andrei Belousov's visit to Pyongyang this week formalized what has been de facto since 2024: North Korea is no longer a supplier — it's an ally. Moscow is negotiating a formal military cooperation agreement for 2027-2031, institutionalizing a partnership that extends beyond the Ukraine war.

Kim Jong Un opened a memorial museum for North Korean soldiers killed fighting in Kursk. Official Russian statements now openly acknowledge Korean troops 'fought shoulder to shoulder with our soldiers' — something previously denied or obscured. An estimated 14,000 North Korean troops deployed to Russia, with South Korean intelligence reporting approximately 2,000 killed.

For us, this is manpower without mobilization — political relief at the cost of dependence on an unpredictable partner. For Pyongyang, it's combat experience, potential access to Russian missile and satellite technology, and leverage in its own confrontation with Seoul and Washington.

The deeper this partnership institutionalizes, the more it locks us into a coalition of the sanctioned. That has strategic value against Western pressure — but it also means our security posture in Northeast Asia becomes tied to Kim's calculus, not ours. We are not the senior partner in this relationship the way we pretend to be with China.
sections.whyItMatters: Our position: We've traded strategic flexibility for immediate manpower relief. The play: Institutionalize the alliance to secure the pipeline. The trap: Pyongyang's nuclear brinkmanship or miscalculation could drag us into crises we didn't choose, on the Korean Peninsula's timeline, not ours.

sections.heading: Iran: Strategic Leverage Without Strategic Control
sections.body:
Putin hosted Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi in St. Petersburg on April 27, praising the Iranian people for 'resistance to the US' and promising Moscow would 'do everything that serves Tehran's and its neighbors' interest.' This is our opening to position Russia as the indispensable mediator in the Iran crisis — and to deepen the anti-Western coalition.

The Hormuz blockade has handed us an oil windfall and a lever against Europe. Iran's isolation makes Tehran more dependent on Moscow, Beijing, and Pyongyang. But we don't control Iran's calculus. Tehran has offered to reopen the Strait in exchange for the US lifting its blockade and ending the war — explicitly postponing nuclear discussions. That's a deal Iran is willing to make without consulting us.

Our support for Iran buys us leverage in the Middle East and a partner against American hegemony. But it also exposes us to escalation dynamics we cannot manage. If the US-Iran conflict intensifies — or if Iran makes a calculation that cuts us out — we are spectators to events that shape our oil revenues, our security environment, and our global positioning.

The deeper truth: We are building a coalition of the constrained. Iran needs us because it has no other options. But Iran also has its own red lines, its own negotiations, and its own capacity to cut deals that leave us holding the bag.
sections.whyItMatters: Our position: The Iran crisis gives us leverage and oil revenue, but we're not the decision-maker. The play: Position Russia as the essential mediator. The trap: Iran cuts a deal with the West that normalizes relations, ends Hormuz disruption, and leaves us isolated with a sanctions-dependent economy.

sections.heading: European Rearmament: The Clock We Can't Stop
sections.body:
The most dangerous structural shift we face isn't happening on the Ukrainian front — it's happening in German factories. Military spending by European NATO members rose faster in 2025 than at any time since 1953. Germany alone spent $114 billion on defense — a 24% year-on-year increase and the first time since 1990 that Berlin crossed the 2% of GDP threshold.

Rheinmetall has increased production capacity for artillery from 70,000 to 1.1 million rounds annually. Germany now has more conventional ammunition production capacity than the United States. Berlin's new defense strategy calls for building 'Europe's strongest land force' within a decade — explicitly framed against us.

Global military spending hit a record $2.89 trillion in 2025. The top three spenders — the US, China, and us — account for 51% of that. But the growth is coming from Europe and Asia. American spending actually declined 7.5% in 2025, largely due to the halt in Ukraine aid — but a new US budget proposal calls for $1.5 trillion in defense spending.

We face a structural problem: The longer this war continues, the more Europe rearms. The more Europe rearms, the more our post-war security environment deteriorates. We are in a race against time — but we are not the ones running fastest.
sections.whyItMatters: Our position: European rearmament is the long-term threat that outlasts any battlefield outcome in Ukraine. The trap: A protracted war accelerates the rearmament we fear; a quick settlement leaves us facing a rearmed Europe anyway. Time is not on our side.

sections.heading: The Sanctions Ecosystem: Evasion Industrialized
sections.body:
Sanctions haven't collapsed our economy. They've reorganized it. A transnational evasion ecosystem now spans Central Asia, the South Caucasus, Turkey, and China — a network of intermediaries, crypto platforms, and logistics hubs with a vested interest in sanctions persistence.

Kyrgyzstan has evolved from entrepôt to financial hub, processing rouble-pegged stablecoins and payments to defense-linked entities. The EU's 20th sanctions package explicitly targeted Russian crypto use as a 'systemic threat,' naming tokens like A7A5 and platforms like Grinex. The EU also imposed anti-circumvention sanctions on Kyrgyzstan itself.

The evasion infrastructure is real — but it comes at a cost: deeper dependency on China, a more militarized lower-productivity economy, and permanent isolation from Western technology and capital. As CSIS notes, sanctions have degraded the technological quality of our military-industrial output without eliminating its capacity to sustain war. We can produce volume; we cannot produce at the technological frontier.

The Iran war's dual Hormuz blockade has actually aided our sanctions position — giving us cost-plus oil economics and leverage over transit states like Kazakhstan. But that's temporary. The ecosystem we've built is resilient but not generative. It lets us survive; it doesn't let us compete.
sections.whyItMatters: Our position: Sanctions evasion has become an industrial ecosystem with stakeholders invested in its perpetuation. The trap: We've traded integration with the global economy for a sanctions-arbitrage network that can't generate growth, only survival.

sections.heading: The Front: Attrition Without End — And Now, Our Air Defenses Are Cracking
sections.body:
Ukraine's long-range strike campaign is exposing a vulnerability we've hidden for two years: our air defense network is overstretched and burning through interceptors faster than we can replace them. Ukraine's commander-in-chief Oleksandr Syrskyi reports we're experiencing shortages of missiles to counter Ukrainian drones and strike assets.

The ISW notes Ukraine is 'exploiting the large attack surface area of Russia's deep rear and overstretched Russian air defenses.' Russian military bloggers themselves warn that 'fabricating tens of thousands of Pantsir missiles out of thin air is physically impossible' and that our antiaircraft forces are being stretched 'to the limit.' Ukraine claims to have destroyed at least half of our Pantsir systems.

Meanwhile, our own recruitment practices have become a liability. African governments are publicly accusing us of deceiving their citizens into frontline combat — Kenya's foreign minister traveled to Moscow in March to protest. African recruits are being used as cannon fodder in human wave attacks. This is not the image we want projected to the Global South we're courting.

We continue to inflict losses — 161 combat engagements in a single day along the front. But we're not advancing. We're holding. And the cost of holding is depleting capabilities — air defense interceptors, trained manpower, industrial capacity — that we cannot replenish at the rate we're expending them.
sections.whyItMatters: Our position: We're trading long-term capability for short-term territorial denial. The play: Attrition works if we can outlast Ukrainian resources and Western support. The trap: We're burning through our own irreplaceable stockpiles — Pantsir missiles take months to produce; Ukraine's drones take days.

sections.heading: Intelligence Exposure: The Network in Lithuania
sections.body:
Ukrainian and European law enforcement have exposed a Russia-linked network that was preparing contract killings, sabotage, and arson across multiple EU countries. The operation, which began in August 2024, was directed by our special services from outside the EU through intermediaries in Lithuania, Latvia, Georgia, Greece, and other states.

Lithuanian authorities have issued suspicions against 13 individuals, detained nine, and issued European arrest warrants for four others. Three suspects arrested abroad have already been extradited. The network targeted a Russian dissident who had received asylum in Lithuania and a Lithuanian citizen known for supporting Ukraine.

This is the price of covert operations: exposure. Every network discovered is years of investment destroyed. Every arrest creates intelligence that flows back to hostile services. Every publicly named intermediary becomes a liability for future operations.

We will continue these operations — they are the leverage we have on European soil. But the operational environment is hardening. European counterintelligence is better resourced and more focused than at any point since the Cold War. The days of easy recruitment and deniable action are over.
sections.whyItMatters: Our position: Covert operations remain a key lever, but the operational environment is increasingly hostile. The trap: Overreliance on deniable operations in a high-awareness environment leads to exposure that undermines both current operations and future capacity.

bottomLine: We have burned through the reserves that gave us strategic resilience. Our war economy is a depletion machine — converting future capacity into present survival, but unable to regenerate what it consumes. The Iran war's oil windfall is a temporary reprieve that masks structural decay; we cannot spend our way out of a supply-side crisis. European rearmament proceeds faster than our ability to counter it; time is working against us there. North Korea and Iran give us coalition partners, but at the cost of dependency and exposure to their miscalculations. The question before the leadership is no longer whether we can win — but what minimum settlement we can secure before our capacity to negotiate at all collapses. We are not adapting. We are depleting. The bill is coming due.
sources: RFE/RL, Reuters, Fortune, NDTV, Kyiv Post, ISW, Business Insider, Stripes/SIPRI, The Asia Live, France24, MoneyLife, The BFT Online, Carnegie Russia Eurasia Center
